perturbation.original_text: Section 4.2. Termination for Convenience. Licensor reserves the right to terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice for any reason, including if the usage of the Brand is not in compliance with the standards and policies.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 4.2. Termination for Convenience. Licensor reserves the right to terminate this Contract immediately upon written notice for any reason, including if the usage of the Brand is not in compliance with the guidelines and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The terms **'Agreement'** is being modified to **'Contract'**, and **'standards and policies'** is being modified to **'guidelines and regulations.'** This introduces uncertainty about whether the terms can be used interchangeably, particularly given the original explicit definition of 'Agreement' at the beginning of the document. This in-text contradiction will affect how termination for convenience can be triggered. The guidelines and regulations may refer to a different set of documents and policies of the Licensor. It will cause confusion on whether or not **standards and policies** and **guidelines and regulations** are the same.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 4.2. Termination for Convenience. Licensor reserves the right to terminate this Contract immediately upon written notice for any reason, including if the usage of the Brand is not in compliance with the guidelines and regulations.
perturbation.justification: The modification replaces 'Agreement' with 'Contract' and 'standards and policies' with 'guidelines and regulations.' Because the document may have a separate definition of 'Agreement' and now a new definition may need to be created for the term 'Contract', it creates ambiguity. Further, the changed terms, 'guidelines and regulations' may refer to a completely different set of requirements than the original 'standards and policies' which may impact the original intended purpose of the clause, and thus the contradicted text. Since the contradicted text also uses the modified language, the changed text directly impacts the contradicted text, which is considered to be a strong, in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Section 7.1. Indemnity by Licensee. Licensee will defend at its expense, indemnify and hold harmless Licensor and its affiliates and its and their respective directors, officers, employees, shareholders, investors, agents and representatives from any losses, liabilities, obligations, damages, awards, settlements, judgments, fees, costs or expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs of suit) arising out of or relating to any third-party Action against any of them that arises out of or relates to (i) any breach by Licensee of this Agreement or its warranties, representations, covenants and undertakings hereunder, (ii) Licensee's operation of the Licensee Business or (iii) any claim that Licensee's use of the Brand, other than as explicitly authorized by this Agreement, Infringes the rights of a third party.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 7.1. Indemnity by Licensee. Licensee will defend at its expense, indemnify and hold harmless Licensor and its affiliates and its and their respective directors, officers, employees, shareholders, investors, agents and representatives from any losses, liabilities, obligations, damages, awards, settlements, judgments, fees, costs or expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs of suit) arising out of or relating to any third-party Claim against any of them that arises out of or relates to (i) any breach by Licensee of this Agreement or its warranties, representations, covenants and undertakings hereunder, (ii) Licensee's operation of the Licensee Business or (iii) any claim that Licensee's use of the Brand, other than as explicitly authorized by this Agreement, Infringes the rights of a third party.
perturbation.explanation: The term **'Action'** is being modified to **'Claim'**. This introduces ambiguity because 'Action' is explicitly defined in Section 2 in relation to legal proceedings, whereas 'Claim' is not explicitly defined and can also relate to different types of legal actions. The term Action is related to third-party Action. It will cause confusion on whether or not Action is referring to Claim, since a Claim can relate to a case, while an Action is related to claim, allegation, action, demand, proceeding or suit. There is also a definition of Action in Section 5 which refers to bringing an Action to remedy.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Licensee acknowledges and agrees that, as between the parties, Licensor is the sole owner of all right, title and interest in and to the Brand. Licensee agrees not to do anything inconsistent with such ownership, including (i) filing to register any trademark or service mark containing the Brand or (ii) directly or indirectly challenging, contesting or otherwise Source: MORGAN STANLEY DIRECT LENDING FUND, 10-12G/A, 11/19/2019 disputing the validity, enforceability or Licensor's ownership of the Brand (and the associated goodwill), including without limitation, in any claim, allegation, action, demand, proceeding or suit ("Action") regarding enforcement of this Agreement or involving any third party.
perturbation.justification: The modification replaces 'Action' with 'Claim'. 'Action' is a defined term in the contradicted text (and Section 2, as noted in the explanation) specifically referring to 'claim, allegation, action, demand, proceeding or suit' related to the Brand and enforcement of the agreement. By changing 'Action' to 'Claim' in Section 7.1, and 'Claim' being undefined, it creates ambiguity and potential contradiction regarding the scope of the indemnity. The contradicted text relies on the specific definition of 'Action', and the change undermines this, leading to a strong in-text contradiction. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition, as the contract now uses 'Claim' where 'Action' was previously used and defined, potentially leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS, Licensee is a closed-end investment company that has elected to be treated as a business development company under the Investment Company Act of 1940, as amended (together with the rules promulgated thereunder) (the "Licensee Business");
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS, Licensee is a closed-end investment company that has elected to be treated as a business development company under the Investment Company Act of 1940, as amended (together with the regulations promulgated thereunder) (the "Licensee Operations");
perturbation.explanation: The terms **'rules promulgated thereunder'** is being modified to **'regulations promulgated thereunder'**, and **'Licensee Business'** is being modified to **'Licensee Operations'**. This introduces uncertainty about whether the licensee business is the same as the licensee operations or if it is just different regulations. Since the original text specifies that Licensee Business is an investment company, it is not clear if the operations of the company are different from the business of it. This in-text contradiction will affect the scope of what activities are subject to the trademark license agreement, or affect the Licensee's operation of the Licensee Business section 7.1.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Section 7.1. Indemnity by Licensee. Licensee will defend at its expense, indemnify and hold harmless Licensor and its affiliates and its and their respective directors, officers, employees, shareholders, investors, agents and representatives from any losses, liabilities, obligations, damages, awards, settlements, judgments, fees, costs or expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and costs of suit) arising out of or relating to any third-party Claim against any of them that arises out of or relates to (i) any breach by Licensee of this Agreement or its warranties, representations, covenants and undertakings hereunder, (ii) Licensee's operation of the Licensee Business or (iii) any claim that Licensee's use of the Brand, other than as explicitly authorized by this Agreement, Infringes the rights of a third party.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Licensee Business" to "Licensee Operations" in the definition creates ambiguity regarding the scope of the indemnity provided in Section 7.1. The original text clearly defines the licensee's activity as a 'business,' while the changed text uses 'operations.' This could lead to a dispute over whether certain activities fall under the indemnity if they are considered 'operations' but not 'business,' or vice versa, creating a strong in-text contradiction. The term 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' applies directly, as the contract now uses two different terms, which could cause conflicting obligations because it is unclear if they are interchangeable.
